title: The Forgotten Artery: Why You SHOULD Know About Zygomaticofacial [Aesthetics Mastery Show]
filename: The Forgotten Artery： Why You SHOULD Know About Zygomaticofacial [Aesthetics Mastery Show].en.vtt
excerpt: I've got one i've never felt you i don't know if you've got one and i don't know how sensitive this test is but i can feel a little notch there i think you might have one too welcome to the aesthetics mastery show i'm dr tim pearce hi i'm miranda pierce and today we're talking ab…

segments.text: i've got one i've never felt you i don't know if you've got one and i don't know how sensitive this test is but i can feel a little notch there i think you might have one too welcome to the aesthetics mastery show i'm dr tim pearce hi i'm miranda pierce and today we're talking about the forgotten artery so it's often left off in textbooks and it's often left off in teaching and when you discover it when you've been injecting for a few years it can make your blood run cold so let's talk about the zygomatica facial artery so why is it not in the textbooks i'm not entirely sure um it might be because it's actually not present on all people so it's a in one paper the best paper i could find on it and it was only mentioned um that it was present in only one in three people okay so it's not always there that's one of the reasons it's just a tiny little artery that comes out of a little foramen so

little foramen so the zygomatic or facial foramen you can sometimes pop hate it i've got one i've never felt you i don't know if you've got one and i don't know how sensitive this test is but i can feel a little notch there i think you might have one too you can sometimes feel it and i think it because it comes with a nerve you can feel it feel slightly different if you push on it that might be a guide i'd really love to have an ultrasound to find out for sure um but that might be one thing that you can do to figure out whether you've got one there of course the reason is so important is that we actually inject very close to it many times so it's important to know that it exists okay so what kind of treatment are we going to come across this for so you might be injecting in this area because you're treating the lateral logic junction so kind of blending in the cheek and the eye or it could be part of a cheek treatment that you're injecting it's often where we're trained to inject on this like on the bone on the zygoma so we're often at bone level when treating cheeks and tear troughs depending on your technique and that's why it's so important to know about its existence so is it like to cause any problems if

you were to inject it well i've never seen a case or heard of a case of it being injected that cause an injury um but that doesn't mean they haven't happened because most of the injuries that we get actually aren't reported or shared so it could have happened and in terms of what the impact would be well it's connect it actually comes off probably a lacrimal artery in many cases and the lacrimal artery comes off the ophthalmic artery so it is theoretically possible if you injected enough product into that space that you could cause blindness that would be the most and it's very theoretical but possibly you could cause that i think in your favor is probably very small in most people so it's probably very hard to hear very hard to cannulate and in most cases it wouldn't make you wouldn't a we wouldn't be able to push a lot of product into it that probably protects people and also if there's a nerve coming from it as well you're likely to get a reaction from the patient and that might give you some protection and because most clinicians would naturally not persist if the patient's in significantly more discomfort than they would be usually but it's a bit disconcerting that we don't know about it i mean

did you just come across it so yeah i've had clinicians basically if you memorize most anatomy posters or most anatomy pictures it's often left off so if you and also the way a lot of the training if you watch an injector illustrate the arteries on the face they often leave that one off and so it when you discover it on your own it's a bit disconcerting i think it's because it's clinically probably not that important but we all like to know what's going on before we inject so when you find out that it's there and you weren't taught it's a little bit of a moment of i wonder what else could be there that i haven't been taught so um it's useful to know that it's there and everyone who watches it does know now so i think part of the confidence of being a fully rounded clinician is to be able to predict a certain amount obviously you don't know everything that's going on underneath but i think if you suddenly get yeah absolutely well we're in a battle constantly to build a mental model that gives us the confidence to actually inject and when you find out there's some fundamental flaw in your model in a part of the face that you regularly inject it's

regularly inject it's it is rightfully disconcerting but the great news is if that's happened to anyone who's listening to this then you're on the way away from that and it's actually a continuous battle we're always picking up little things about anatomy that you hadn't quite visualized correctly or you discover there's a variant of it and this is the process you should be on your whole career is finding out a little bit of information improving your mental model and then injecting slightly differently so can you show us on bruce the head yeah so it's just this little vessel just about there i don't know if that's gonna be good enough to crop in on um and there's usually a foramen so if you google zygomatic of facial foramen you will find it um in some skulls but you'll also see in many skulls it's not present and this is where the confusion comes it just isn't present on all people and on anatomical skulls it's not always present but it could be present on your patient i would also then get say if you've done some exercise i've recommended this a lot of times before if you think you might have one i'm pretty sure i can feel a pulse on mine obviously we have pulse on our fingers too but if i go for a run

and my heart's already pounding and i put my finger on these various places i can often feel a little pulse and that will make it more real to you exactly where it is in real life rather than just on textbooks so how can people avoid this artery when injecting so to avoid the psychomatic facial artery the first thing is to palpate for it get it especially in thinner people you might be able to feel the little a little foramen and that'll give you a good guide where not to inject um the next thing would be as always if you're using a needle in that area you should aspirate but i would also suggest that you with some practice you'll get an idea about where it is then you can just go either side of it and then aspirate and watch your patient because they if you touch on the area and there's a nerve there they will respond differently so i always say this the patient responds differently to how they are to other injections you might just want to find another spot even if it's four millimeters over to the left or the right and they don't respond in the same way that gives you some reassurance um finally you might want to use different instruments if you know you're injecting around there for example a

cannula is probably less likely to cause injury than a needle in this circumstance and yeah that should be it so would different fillers affect the risk level for this artery well i'm only thinking from first principles here because we really don't know but it does make sense to me that a lower viscosity product would go through smaller vessels more easily and i know if you depending on which products you use they often come with thinner needles so there is if you're using like i use driven ball bella quite a lot great product but if i'm using that with a 31 gauge needle it's likely to flow a bit more easy into those vessels if i was to cannulate one so i think you're a bit more likely to cause a significant occlusion with a low viscosity product where you're injecting in that area so that's not to say that they're dangerous or that you should always use thicker quality products it's just a principle around what's most likely to flow deeper into the vasculature and it's low viscosity products so let us know if this is news to you or if you already knew about the zygomatic of facial artery

of facial artery don't forget that if you enjoyed this youtube show and you would like to hear it in podcast form please do head over to spotify or itunes or wherever you get your podcast and listen to us on your drive or you commute and if you're already listening to it you can get the visual version on youtube indeed thank you very much for watching take care see you next week [Music] you
